div.p: Colors: Header/Meta=Purple · Data/Queue=Blue · Sync=Orange · Padding=Gray · Pointers=Orange · Hot-line=Red

g.text: `work_item_t` (24 B — fits in ⅓ cache line)

tr.td: Acquires Modified; all other cores → Invalid
Sends RFO; prev owner flushes to L3; acquires Modified
Every enqueue+dequeue pair causes 2 RFO round-trips on the
inter-core interconnect, even though head and tail are logically
independent. A 64-B pad between them eliminates this at the cost of

g.text: end: 0x18 = 24 B total · fits 2.67× per cache line

div.p: cache line 0 (0x0000–0x003F, 64 B) holds queue[0], queue[1], and 16 B of queue[2]

g.text: offset 0x6000 ── HOT CONTROL CACHE LINE (line 384) ── ⚠ FALSE-SHARE ZONE ──
0x6000 │ head │ int (4 B) ← consumer writes here
0x6004 │ tail │ int (4 B) ← producer writes here
0x600C │ (pad) │ 4 B — alignment before pthread_mutex_t (40 B needs 8-B align)

div.p: ⚠ MESI False-Sharing Hot-Spot head (consumer writes) and tail (producer writes) share cache line 384. Every write by either thread sends an RFO (Request for Ownership), invalidating the other core's L1 copy → inter-core bus traffic. Fix for high-throughput use: pad head and tail to separate 64-B cache lines: c int head; char _pad0[60]; int tail; char _pad1[60];
(Not done here; mutex serialises access anyway.)

g.text: 0x60A0 │ threads[0..15] │ pthread_t[16] = 128 B (8 B each, lines 386–387)
0x6120 │ thread_count │ int (4 B) — number of live worker threads
